text: Good day and thank you for standing by. Welcome to the ASML 2026 First Quarter Financial Results Conference Call on April 15th, 2026. At this time all participants are in a listen only mode. After the speaker's introduction there will be a question and answer session. To ask a question during the session you will need to press star 1 and 1 on your telephone. You will then hear an automated message advising your hand is raised. To withdraw your question please press star 1 and 1 again. Please be advised that today's conference is being recorded. I would now like to hand the conference call over to Mr. Jim Cavanaugh. Please go ahead. Thank you operator. Welcome everyone. This is Jim Cavanaugh head of investor relations at ASML. Joining me today on the call are ASML CEO Christoph Fouquet and our CFO Roger Daffa. The subject of today's call is ASML 2026 First Quarter Results. The link to the call will be 60 minutes and questions will be taken in the order in which they are received. This call is also being broadcast live over the internet at www.ASML.com. A transcript of management's opening remarks and replay of the call will be available on our website shortly following the conclusion of this call. Before we begin I would like to caution listeners that comments made by management during this conference call will include forward looking statements within the meaning of the federal securities laws. These forward looking statements involve material risks and uncertainties. For discussion of risk factors I encourage you to review the safe harbor statement contained in today's press release and the presentation found on our website at www.ASML.com and in ASML's annual report on form 20F and other documents as filed with the Securities and Exchange Commission. With that I would like to turn the call over to Christoph Fouquet for a brief introduction. Mr. Jim welcome everyone and thank you for joining us for our First Quarter 2026 Resource Conference call. Before we begin the Q&A session, Fouquet and I would like to provide an overview and some commentaries on the First Quarter 2026 results as well as provide some additional comments on the current business environment and on our future business. Thank you Christoph and welcome everyone. I will first review the First Quarter 2026 financial accomplishments and then provide guidance on the second quarter of 2026. Let me start with our First Quarter accomplishments. In the First Quarter of 2026 total net sales were 8.8 billion euros which is within our guidance. Net system sales were at 6.3 billion euros which includes over 4.1 billion euros from EV system sales including sales from two high NA systems and over 2.1 billion euros from non EV system sales. Net system sales were almost equally split between logic at 49% and memory at 51%. Installed base management sales for the quarter of K-Men at 2.5 billion euros slightly above our guidance. The first margin for the quarter was at the high end of our guidance at 53% primarily due to the contribution of very high margin components within our installed base business. On operating expenses we came in as guidance. Our new expenses were rounded at 1.2 billion euros and as the GNA expenses came in at 0.3 billion euros. The effective tax rates for Q1 was 17.1%. For the full year 2026 the expected annualized effective tax rate is around 17%. Net income in Q1 was 8.8 billion euros representing 31.4% of total net sales and resulting in earnings per share of 7.15 euros. Turning to the balance sheet we ended the First Quarter with cash, cash equivalents and short-term investments at a level of 8.4 billion euros. Our Q1 free cash grow was negative, 2.6 billion euros largely driven by the timing of down payment. With that I would like to turn to our expectations for the second quarter of 2026. We expect Q2 total net sales to be between 8.4 billion euros and 9 billion euros. We expect our Q2 installed base management sales to be around 2.5 billion euros. First margin for Q2 is expected to be between 51 and 52%. The expected R&D expenses for Q2 are around 1.2 billion euros and as the GNA is expected to be around 0.3 billion euros. Moving to our cash return to our shareholders in Q1 ASM outpaid a third interim dividend over 2025 of 1.60 euro per ordinary share. Is Malentans declared total dividend for the year 2025 of 7.50 euro per ordinary share which is a 17% increase compared to 2024. Recognizing the three interim dividends of 1.60 euro per ordinary share paid in 25 and 26, this needs to a final dividend proposal to the annual general meeting of 2.70 euro per ordinary share. In Q1 2026 we purchase shares for total amount of around 1.1 billion euros and with that I would like to turn the call back over to Christoph. Thank you, Roshi. As Roshi highlighted we finished the first quarter with good financial reserves. Looking ahead with respect to the market. The growth outlook for the semiconductor industry continues to solidify driven primarily by AI related infrastructure investment. These investments are increasing demand for advanced logic and member of chips in many areas and for the foreseeable future demand will continue to outpace supply. This creates constraints across end markets from AI to mobile and PC's which is driving our customers to aggressively add capacity. In the memory business many customers have called that they are sold out for the remaining of the year and that they expect the supply limitation to persist beyond 2026 despite their plans to add significant capacity. In the logic business our customers are adding capacity across multiple advanced nodes to support demand while continuing to ramp the two nanometer node in support of next generation HPC and mobile application. They also expect supply limitation across those advanced nodes beyond 2026. Also memory and logic customers are responding to this unprecedented demand by increasing capital expenditures and accelerating capacity expansion plans this year and beyond. Those investments are supported by long term agreement with their own customer. In addition to expanding capacity both advanced DRAM and logic customers continue to further adopt EV and immersion DPV on new process nodes which further increases their demand for lithography. As a resource ISMR order intake continues to be very strong and we stay closely aligned with our customers to support their needs. At the same time we offer customers productivity upgrade for their install base to increase their short term output requirements. Turning to our capacity expansion plan for 2026 we are executing on an output plan of at least 60 low NA UV system and despite a slow start as discussed last quarter driving output of our immersion system close to that in 2025. Based on these developments in demand and order intake we are quarter by quarter increasing the move rates for our HPM product raising low NA UV capacity to at least 80 system next year and scaling DPV and application products in alignment. We continue to see a strong year ahead and expect ISMR to grow in 2026. Therefore, we are updating our 2026 guidance both narrowing and increasing the expected revenue range to between 36 billion euros and 40 billion euros while maintaining our expectation of a gross margin between 51% and 53%. We are revenue weighted to the second half of the year. As we said last quarter last quarter UV revenue is expected to raise significantly this year driven by the dynamics in advanced logic and the RAM markets mentioned earlier. For non-EU revenue we previously expected this to be similar to last year but given continued demand momentum we now expect gross as a resource of customer adding more DPV lithography to support their expansion plans. We also expect install base management revenue to grow significantly this year driven by service revenue from our expanding UV install base and customer demand for performance upgrades to support their increasing capacity requirements. We expect that the bandwidth in our 2026 guidance accommodates potential outcomes of ongoing discussions around export control. Turning to technology we continue to make very good progress on this front with several developments recently highlighted at the STIE Advanced lithography and Patterning Conference this February in San Jose. At the conference we presented an updated low NAIV productive roadmap that reflects improvement to both our short term and long term plans for these products. This includes the ability to reach at least 330 wafer per hour on low NAIV at the start of the next decade enabled in large part by our continued source powered improvement as evidenced by our recent 1000 WAT source demonstration. In the short term all NxE3800 E system can benefit from an upgrade that provides an increase of 10 wafer per hours with 230 wafer per hours available immediately to all customer. For NxE3800 F system we have raised the wafer per hour specification from 250 wafer to 260 wafer per hours. We plan to start shipping that system in 2027 with full volume slated for 2028. We also shared that the INE platform has now processed over half a million wafers and achieved over 80% availability. So at the conference we saw customers presenting several strong papers highlighting the progress with INE. These presentations demonstrated use cases in both logic and in DRAM in which a single INE exposure can replace complex multi-patterning processes that today require free or fall low NA exposures. For some critical layers INE can reduce the number of opposite steps by a factor of 10. The progress being made throughout the ecosystem especially with resist allows us to target line and speed pitches of 18 nanometer for logic and contact pitches below 28 nanometer for DRAM. This means that INE can support single exposed for at least three nodes in logic and DRAM. We continue to work to further mature the platform alongside our customer as they start to test the technology on product wafers and move closer to bringing INE into high volume manufacturing. Looking more broadly what we have seen over the past few months has further confirmed our view on the positive impact of AI on customer demand for our advanced product and especially for our EVC stem in both advanced memory and logic. We see the end market dynamic supporting a shift in product mix towards more demand for our advanced lithography product and an increase in nether intensity. Our strong productivity on map on low NA in combination with the introduction of INE supports for the cost of technology reduction for our customer. In summary demand remains strong for the entire product portfolio. With that we will be happy to take your questions. Thank you Raje and thank you Christoph. The operator will instruct you momentarily on the protocol for the Q&A session. Beforehand I would like to ask you to kindly limit yourself to one question with one short follow up if necessary. This will allow us to get through as many callers as possible. Now operator could we have your final instructions and then the first question please. Thank you. As a reminder to ask a question you will need to press star one and one on your telephone and wait for your name to be announced. To withdraw your question please press star one and one again. We will now take the first question and your first question today comes from the line of Joe Khatriki from Wells Fargo. Please go ahead. Yeah thanks for taking the question. Maybe I just start with the updated 2026 revenue guide. Do you talk about taking up your emerging outlook and your DUV outlook? How much of that is coming from China versus non-China and did the EUV outlook change at all? It's a high growth for Jay. This is primarily non-China when it comes to emerging. China remains at the midpoint around 20%. That's the expectation that we have for the China business so that view has not changed. As we mentioned on the last call we were looking at immersion and said given the supply chain situation and immersion we said we doubt whether we can get immersion to the level that we had last year. We've been working extremely hard and right now we're at a point where we believe we can get immersion close to the levels that we had last year. That would primarily flow to the non-Chinese customer. That's where that is. That's a major driver of the uptick and also in EUV. We believe there is a bit more that can be done. It's in that combination and as you can see from our achievements and also our expectations for the insult based business that one is strong as well. I would say it's actually a combination but clearly immersion is an important part of the uptake of the guidance. That's helpful. Then just as a follow up on the gross margin, the range not changing but clearly immersion increasing. I know that's a bit higher gross margin. Just any put and take there while the gross margin guides say it's the same for 26. Yeah, you're right. I mean for the entire year the gross margin remains in spite of a bit more immersion. But it's also clear that we're increasing the move rate quite a bit. Of course increasing the move rate means that you're hiring people, hiring people, training people that also cost a bit of money. So every ramp you always have a little bit of cost before you have the benefit of that. That's the other side of the equation. That's why we believe that the guidance range as we have it taken into consideration all the puts and takes, we believe keeping it as we articulated it as the water would go is the right way to go. Thank you. You're welcome. Thank you. Your next question comes from the line of Chris Sankar from TD Cowan. Please go ahead. We have time to take my question. I do have them first run, Rosie O'Kirstoff. I'm kind of curious, what is the visibility you're getting from your customers? Does that extend into 2028? Based on how to think about ASM as good profile in 27 and 28, either qualitatively or quantitatively? Well, I think 2028 is still quite far away. And I think a lot of the discussion we have with our customer today are still a bit about 2026. As Rosie mentioned, we're still working hard even for 2026 with our customer to make sure we deliver what they need. And of course, a lot of discussion are moving to 2027, which is also why we are also there working very hard to increase the capacity. But 2028 is a bit too far away, I think. Is there a way to think about 27 at this moment, Chris Starfall? Well, I think we mentioned already that we are growing our move rate every quarter. So I think we are still in a dynamic where we believe that we have to create more space for our customer. I think we've explained in our introduction that we are preparing to build at least 80 low-end U.V. system next year. So I think these are the sign, if you need any, that of course the discussion we have about 2027 are currently around making sure that we have enough capacity for our customer. Carl, another quick follow-up. I think you mentioned how you are undershipping demand today. If you start looking at 2780 unit capacity for low NA, are you meeting demand? Are you still undershipping demand at 80 units next year? Well, I think on those discussion, and I think the number we share today are, I will say, the results of very close discussion with our customers. We talked about at least 80 because those discussions are still ongoing. I think you have seen, of course, the dynamic in the last couple of quarters. So I think we said that also last quarter. We were working every day very closely with our customer to make sure basically that we stay in line with what they need. I think we have, of course, a very strong mission to meet their demand. So that's what those number reflects. I think that's the results of where our discussion are today. Thank you, Kristoff. You're welcome. Thank you. Your next question comes from the line of C.D. Me is from Contell. It's Gerald. Please go ahead. Yeah, good morning, Good afternoon. Thank you for taking the question. I guess first question going back to Gross margins. And if we isolate March versus June, March, your revenue to INA zero, I think it embedded in June. And so I get kind of the upgrades as a real positive for March, but I would have thought the higher low in EUV and immersion would have led to higher gross margins. I understand you're adding Edgout, but that still seems pretty severe. So wondering if there's anything else in that mix that's driving that change. No, there's nothing in the mix really CJ. It really is down to the elements that you just referred to. So a few put on takes, puts on takes, but the primary ones being the very high margin elements in the upgrade business that we had in Q1. We're not counting on such a strong pattern in the upgrade business in Q2. So that's an important element. And then indeed, to the point that I made earlier on, the increase in hub counts that were going through to prepare for optics for upgrades in the move rate. So it's pretty low elements as a result of which we're now narrowing the bandwidth for this particular quarter to 51 and 52. Of course, you would see that the second half of the year, that volume is more skewed towards the second half of the year, right? If you look at our adorac items, so it's also appropriate to see more of the benefits of the increase of the ramp that we're doing in that in the second half of the year. As a result of that, we maintain the guidance for the full year at 51 to 53%. Perfect mix, and I guess as a follow up, Kristoff, to try to talk about the supply side, obviously under shipping and demand today. Curious, how you working internally at ASML outside of headcount? What kind of things are you doing to help drive productivity and extra supply and also with your supply chain, particularly around the optics and mirror fronts? What is necessary there? And if you are considering 80 plus EUV, what does that mean from an immersion unit perspective in 27? Thanks so much. I think we're working on many, many fronts, I would say. You mentioned the supply chain, I think. We have been mostly explaining the last few years that we were preparing the supply chain basically to be able to go to a capability of 90 and low NA and to a capability of 604 for DPUV, total DPUV. And I think what we see in these frames is that a lot of the preparation is paying off. So I think that, of course, you always have challenge with the supply chain, but I will say so far, also, our supply chain has been able to support our move rate increase quarter by quarter. And that's included by the way, Zeiss, to name them. That's included the optic, where I think we had major challenges a few years ago in the previous frames. I think we are in a much better shape there. I think that's also true with what we have prepared in terms of capacity here in ISML. So I'm talking about the space we need to build the tools, both EUV and DPUV. And this allows, as Roger mentioned, that to basically now add the people we will need to meet the move rate that is required. The other element I'd like to stress, we also talked a lot about the 38 and the E in the last few years. As you have noticed, the matching tool is a much better place than it was a year ago, which also allows us to make great progress on cycle time in our factory, and of course, a shorter cycle time gives us opportunity for more tools. So we have many, many parameters that we drive, I would say, all at the same time, very aggressively, basically, to get more output. That's true for EUV. That's true for DPUV. 27 is a bit far for DPUV. So I think that the expectation, as I mentioned before, is that the number of DPUV tools, the number of immersion tools, the demand there, will scale pretty much with what we see on EUV. I think CJ, it's also important to recognize CJ that and Christoph said it. We are working with customers to understand what the capacity requirements are that they have. We're very much focusing on unit numbers. I would say there is a lot more that we're doing to give customers their productivity. First off, and Christoph said it, the maturity of the tool is going up, but also the productivity of the tool is going up. So the fact that we're now looking at a tool from 220 to 230, a wavers per hour, as you know, in 2027, we're going to introduce the F model that gets you 260 wavers per hour. So you have the unit numbers that everyone is very much focused on, but then you should also look at the properties that we're making in terms of productivity. And if you take that into consideration, last year we had 44 tools. If you're just looking at 80 tools, we say at least 80, but if you just look at 80 tools, those 80 tools give you double the way for our capacity that we would have shipped in 2025. And on top of that, we're helping customers upgrade their installed days. So as Christoph said, we're really working hand in glove with the customers to look at what is your capacity need, what's the easiest way and also the most economical way for you to get to the productivity that you need. And we're executing on all fronts on availability, on productivity, on unit numbers, capacity, and on upgrading the installed days. And last one I want to make, because Roger, make a very good point or so on those productivity upgrades, which you heard in the introduction we have accelerated. I think our customers are extremely happy with that because these allow them to get capacity right away. When you buy new system, you have to wait a bit. In this case, I think this is reflected in the strength of the install base sales, this is something we can provide immediately most of the time, by the way, with the software switch and some qualifications. So I think what is right, I think in our case capacity is a lot more than one pony trick. We feel quite blessed that we have all of these because we have many, many tools we can use to basically make sure we deliver what our customer needs. You're welcome. Thanks. Thank you. Your next question comes from a line of Sunday, best Sunday from JP Morgan. Please go ahead. Yeah, hi. Thanks for letting me on the question. My first question is regarding your DPV capacity. I mean, when you look at it, Christoph, I mean, in the last up cycle, you sold a lot more DPV outside China than you are selling in the last few years. As you go into the next build cycle, don't you think that you will need to add more DPV capacity if China remains at these sort of levels that you're seeing? I'm not sure, Senate, what do you mean by the next build cycle? What are you referring to? What I mean is in 227, when you look into 27 where you're seeing these 80 V tools at the minimum that you're going to ship, if you see the number of DPV tools you sold for the whatever number of EV tools you sold in the last big capacity build cycle, because there's not been a lot of massive capacity building that you're now going to see in the industry over the next two, three years, would you say that you would need more DPV capacity because you have about 600 tool capacity at this point? Is that enough for you in terms of DPV? Well, I think 600 is for total DPV, and I think that when we look at the total, I think we still feel pretty good about that, and a bit like UV, you know, we have other means to play with that number if needed. When it comes to immersion, I think, well, we believe that for non-Chinese customers, the demand on immersion will scale with the demand for UV, because as you know, there is a pretty clear relationship between the two, and if you had UV capacity, you would be also adding some immersion capacity. So I would say we pay for sure the same amount of attention to our DPV ramp, our immersion ramp that we do on UV, because the two are, of course, extremely linked, especially when it comes to non-China. Thanks, Mr. Saff. I mean, maybe Raje, I would like to go back to this question of mix and ASP. I mean, you have indicated for this year that you've got more 3,600, there are some 3,600 shipping this year versus next year, there should not be any 3,600, there should also be this next generation of E and then the F which starts shipping next year. How should we be looking at the mix of UV tools that you ship next year and how that will drive, you know, your revenue growth into UV next year? So this year, as you say, you know, the lion's share is still east, right? So there is a bit of these in there, but the lion's share is clearly still east. And I would say about 20% maybe is east and the rest is east. For next year, I think hardly is any east. And I would say the lion's share of it is east. There will, of course, we ask, we will introduce the F, but just the way customers are planning to ramp their nodes. I believe that the lion's share of the tools by 4,20,27 will still be. So there will be ease at that point with the high throughput that we just talked about, the 230, that will go X factory at that level. And there will be a number of Fs, but that will be a clear money. So would you say that your FPs will grow next year because of how the mix is shifting from this year to next year? Oh, yes. Yeah, clearly. The mix next year is more favorable than this year. No, no these and a bunch of Fs. So clearly the ASP next year will improve over this year. Thank you. You're welcome. Thank you. There is obviously a very high demand in advanced logic and memory. And I was wondering if high and air could play a role in that tight market as high and air could save low and capacity. And necessarily, if high and air can actually replace with the mask. So I just wondering if it could help adoption, in other words, high and air with the tightness of the market? Yeah. Yeah, I think Francois, I think it's a bit too early to answer the question. I think what we see today is that several of our customers are going to test INA on real product with her. So it's basically, they demonstrate for themselves the ability to use this tool on real product. We see that with some logic customer. We see that with some DRAM customer. And, you know, especially when it comes to DRAM, I will say the threshold to start using INA on existing product is pretty low. So I think that's a possibility. I think that, you know, as things evolve in the next few months, accommodation of, again, capacity requirement that continue to be strong plus progress on INA. I will not exclude that. I think it's just a bit too early to respond to the question via the affirmative today. Thank you, Kristoff. And maybe, Roger, it's a follow-up on this F model and maybe the ISP. So, you know, we're not negotiating in this call with our customers, but I think the proxy approach that you just followed doesn't lead you to a very bad outcome. Thank you very much. Thank you. Your next question today comes from the line of Alexander Duvall from Goldman Sachs. Please go ahead. Yes. Good afternoon. Many thanks for the question. A year ago, I think there was some discussion in the market about a market structure for customers where ASML had essentially one very significant customer in the foundry side and two others lagging significantly. And as a result, we started to create all kind of activities in ASML in order to support our customers there. You talked about hybrid bonding. I think we also announced that we were entering, you know, advanced packaging with the XC260. This has been an important tool. We continue to see good traction there. And on top of that, we're looking again at a few more things. Some of them have to do with lithography in advanced packaging. And for process such as hybrid bonding, I think we are continuing to look at the opportunity to support our customer in their activity. And that can be done in many different forms. I think right now we're looking still at a limited amount of use, practically a hybrid bonding, especially in front-end. So not too much activity with our customer. But I will say some discussion for the future on how maybe we could help there. So that's the first part. Yeah, back to the question you had on the Foundry business and the potential for Samsung and Intel. It's pretty clear that the demand in the Foundry business is huge and is outweighing the supply, right? So that leaves a bit of room for others than the market leader. And I think that's room that the other players are trying to enter into. We all know about the plans of Samsung and Taylor, so that's real. And of course that also requires shipment for us, which is happening. The US player in this business already has quite some capacity, I would say. So we've said it before that for this year we're not counting on a huge number of shipments in that regard because they already have quite a bit of capacity. Then you ask about the longer term. Of course the market that is characterized by multiple players, at least will guarantee innovation. And that I think is what is important. I think the market leader has been tremendously innovative. So you cannot say that even in the market that was dominated by one player, you would still see a lot of innovation. I think that's what we've seen in the past couple of years. But having three players in there will probably guarantee even more innovation. And I ultimately think that that is good for the ecosystem. Hi, thanks. Thank you. Our next question today comes from the line of Chris Carter from Wolf Research. Please go ahead. Yes, thank you. Much has been discussed on the call about your own actions to increase your capacity to supply. It could just speak about your customers capacity constraints, specifically the clean room constraints as well. And I guess this is perhaps a better question for next year perhaps. But to what extent is your ability to ship constrained by your own ability to produce tools, your customers' clean room space, or is demand going to be constrained? Is it going to be a supply constrained environment or a demand constrained environment? Well, I think we talked quite a bit about that last quarter, where I think we saw a lot of demand coming in. So this was a bit coming very, very fast. I think we mentioned that the clean room capacity was one of the factor, if not the key factor, on what we could do this year. I think you have seen us increasing our range for 2026, which means that we have progress there. We have even increased a per part of the range. So I think that we see that the plan for 2026 are really solidifying, getting more and more clarity about the number of pedestal that will be available when. So that's clear. I mean, wake up 2027, I think that, well, you know, there is supply limitation right now. I think I mentioned before, we see our customer having a lot less agitation to really accelerate their capital expenditure. I think you, of course, have noticed that our DRAM customer are doing extremely well thanks to the price of memory right now. I think Roger just mentioned for logic or so that, you know, the capacity limitation is quite high. So I would say there is no agitation whatsoever in the mind of our customer at this point of time, not only to invest, also because sometimes they even get guarantee from their own customer on the investment, but also as a resource to move as quickly as possible. So that's the dynamic we are in. And I think that's why we see our outlook improving this year and we continue to have, I would say, very, very active discussion with our customer to prepare next year. But I would say right now everyone is really moving towards, you know, the more the faster the better. Right. That's clear. As a follow up to that, you know, given what's happening in DRAM now, could you speak to what that means for LIFO intensity? And you know, we've seen one of the remedies for the tight supply is migrating to some of the new nodes. You know, how does that affect LIFO intensity and does it have any effect on high- and A adoption at your DRAM customers in an effort to address some of the supply constraints? Well, I think first for DRAM, I think, you know, DRAM has been a bit the perfect storm for ISML because of course we have this capacity build-up. But as we mentioned a few times, we have seen a major adoption of UV in DRAM in 2025. And you may have noticed that our, I would say, U.S. DRAM customer also made this announcement that they were shifting also pretty strongly on UV. And the reason for that is, of course, performance, but it's also capacity because if you are going to use more UV layers, you are going to need less multipatterning and multipatterning takes a lot of space also in the FAPs. I think this is also definitely another argument in favor of UV. I think this was mentioned, by the way, by this U.S. customer in their call. So I would say the first is also that is first more adoption of low-NA UV, which I think is also translated into the number Roshi was mentioning before with, I would say, a very high point on our memory business this year. That's also true for UV. Now of course what's true for low-NA today, I think we expect to be true for INN to feature. So this is again not prime time for INN today, but I can only say that more low-NA UV adoption today can only help for more INN adoption in the future because the logic of INN is the same. It's going to single expose, simplifying the process, getting more space, et cetera, et cetera. So I think DRAM has been really a good story when it comes to lethal intensity in 25, and I think it's translating very strongly into UV demand this year and most of the years to come. Clear, thank you. You're welcome. Thank you. Your next question today comes from the line of timey key from Paramburg. Please go ahead. Hi, thank you for taking my question. So, hopefully on the DRAM sector or memory sector in general. So we have learned that the memory sector has been all siding those three to five years agreement. So with your customer's visibility higher on their own and market, do you see any behavioral change from your site either more willing to give you more visibility of order for your tools going forward and also at the same time because of their currently desperate for capacity? Do you see any room for charge more or upgrade more, i.e. your own business or memory business will be more profitable for you and high visibility than it was previously. Yeah, so in terms of visibility and behavioral change, the visibility to us, customers are very, very open. By the way, that's also the case on the logic side. And it's very, it's very open to us and they're very openly discussing with us also their expansion plans for this year but also beyond. It's also clear that if you look at the scale of the FADs that they're building, it's pretty significant, right? So if you look at the more recent FADs that are being built by our customers, it's pretty clear that they allow for quite some expansion. So if you talk about behavioral change, I think that's probably the number one behavioral change out there. Visivir, they're just very, very open in terms of how they see the market and how they see their further capacity expansion. Now in our model of pricing, as you know, our model of pricing is not based on, you know, the squeeze that our customers find ourselves in. That's not the way we do business. The way we do business is that we look at the value that we provide to our customers, you know, generation on generation tool on tool and we take our fair share in that. And you know, you might say in the current climate, you know, can't you squeeze out a little bit more? I understand that. But it's also true that, you know, when the market goes down a little bit and the customers are going through more difficult times, that it also pays these DCDCs. So, you know, fundamentally, we believe that the model that we have is a fair model. It's also a model that is fair to all the players because I would find it difficult to explain why we're charging more in, let's say, the memory environment versus the logic environment, that's just not the way we do business. So, we're very, very happy with the business model that we have, which is based on the value of our tools and we would gladly continue with that approach. Okay, thank you. And next one is on your AT, at least AT capacity for 2027. So, your at least, if I understand correctly, is the AT is based on what customer have told you for the time being. And is that at least based on customer is limited by clear room space so that they don't know and don't want to commit further or something else? I was driving that at least. At least is, you know, the alignment that we currently have with customers, which takes on board their, you know, their gauge of the market situation, their gauge of the progress that are having in FAB building and, you know, our gauge of the ability to, what's the right way to give them capacity? I told you, unit numbers is one way to provide capacity, but there is more. So, this really is the alignment that we currently have based on the signals that we're having based on the signals that they're providing us and what we think is the appropriate way to give them that capacity. Okay, thank you. You're welcome. Thank you. Your next question today comes from the line of Lee Simpson from Morgan Stanley. Please go ahead. Great. Good morning. Good afternoon. Thanks for squeezing me in. Maybe just a quick clarification question on the install based management. I just want to understand, while I assume at least that most all of the performance in Q1 margin came from that. That's larger because I think you said earlier, there was a high incidence of upgrades business, that quarter, and that most, if not all of the rest of the year will be more of the maintenance type of business. Thanks. No, that would not be fair. So, we expect to, we expect upgrades business to continue throughout the year, but even in the upgrade business, you have different margin profiles of the upgrade services that you provide. And it just happens to be that the business in the install based business that we had in Q1 just happened to be the kind of business that is associated with very, very high growth margins. No, no, we definitely expect the install based business and the upgrade business in that install based business to continue. That's also what you would expect in an environment like this, where as we said before, you know, customers are really asking for upgrades. But, you know, even within the upgrade business, you have the other ones, primarily software based, which come with very high growth margins, and then ones with maybe a little bit less growth margins. That's the mix effect within the install based that we were calling out. Maybe just on that mixed effect, it does look a little more and more the sort of warranty-like business that you have now with install based and EUV is coming through. So how much of a gap are we seeing closure between what is traditional maintenance and what is traditional upgrade cycle? Are we talking about less than 10 percentage points gap between those two elements now? But when you say 10 percentage point, you mean in terms of gross margin or what? In terms of gross margin, yeah. In terms of gross margin. I cannot say that, Lee. It's too diverse. As I just mentioned, the margin profile is very, very diverse. So it's very much dependent on what kind of upgrade to upgrade business. I mean, on the service front, we've given you updates in previous years on the progress that we're making on EUV service gross margin. So, you know, four or five years ago, we were bleeding money. We actually have negative gross margins, and gradually we were able to improve that. And we're now at a gross margin that, you know, is no longer, you know, no longer very much apart from, I would say, the corporate gross margin. So we've gotten that, I think, to a level which I think is distance-wise, the right level for us to aspire to on the upgrade business that very much depends on what it is. So the profile is so different that it would not be appropriate for me to give you a number there. That makes sense. I mean, maybe we could take a step back and look again at the capacity as a multi-year proposition here, because clearly we are going into a massive upcycle with AI. And now we've got a gentike AI and driving to a different whole discussion on what the auto-i could be in and around AI. So our sense here is that EUV is becoming a bottleneck as other tools are in the system. And if we look at your own cleanroom build-out, it did look as though you took maybe, and correct me if I'm wrong, took maybe two or three years to get this set up, which was sensible, given the positive of demand at the time. But could we see this accelerating? Could we see, you know, a concrete poor shell build one year, and then the next year fit out? And it happened consecutively whereby you could have two or three cleanroom projects at the same time, which I guess is me asking, you know, how much land bank can we secure in the immediate vicinity? Well, I think the first thing I'd like to say is that we do not want EUV to be the bottleneck. So I think I'd like to say that very, very strongly. And as we discussed a bit before, we have many, many different ways to drive capacity up. Being, of course, the number of tools we manufacture, which also depend on our cycle time, the speed at which we manufacture them, productivity, et cetera, et cetera. And of course, you know, adding more footprint is another one. And I will say that we will use whatever is needed in the future to meet our customer demand. And we are reviewing this very carefully on a monthly basis where some, as you know, long-term visibility because of the lead time of our tools. And today I think we are not in such a situation, and we have the ability, again, to use any of the tools we just discussed to make sure we continue. And that's something we'll continue to do. So I would just say, again, very strongly, we do not want to be the bottleneck for our customer. And so far, we have been using all the tools we have at hand to make sure we don't get there. And we continue to do that. And the key thing that we've done, Lee, on that front, this is a process that is to get the long lead time items in place. That's what we've done. That gives us many degrees of freedom, including, you talked about the land bank, including making sure that we have sufficient, you know, sufficient room to expand. You know, we've worked on that extremely hard in this region to get that accomplished. And we've recently able to, you know, to get that done. So we have a lot of the greed of freedom on the go-forward basis, with the long lead time items in place. And that gives us the kind of flexibility that that press office is talking about to give customers what we think they need. Yeah, and maybe to illustrate that, if you compare a bit what we did in 25, which is what we could do in basically 27 with the at least 80 system, we more than double basically the total UV capacity we can ship to our customers. This is down basically in about two years. And we do that, I would say, without blinking too much. So we're still on top of our game there. And I think, again, the combination of all the elements we gave you is really putting us in a good position to continue to grow, and also to continue to do that, because this has been some time you concern when the market was not so good, without spending too much money. I think that's also important. So I know that question of bottleneck comes back very often. I think we don't feel at all that we are the bottleneck today. We're very closely working with our customer. And again, we have many, many, many tools in our hands to make sure we keep it this way. Thanks so much. Well done in the quarter. Thank you very much. Okay. I'm afraid that is all the time we have for today. So on behalf of ASML, I would like to thank you all for joining us today. Operator, if you could formally conclude the call, I would appreciate it. Thank you. This concludes the ASML 2026 First Quarter Financial Results Conference call. Thank you for participating. You may now disconnect. Thank you.

segments.text: Good day and thank you for standing by.
Welcome to the ASML 2026 First Quarter Financial Results Conference Call on April 15th, 2026.
At this time all participants are in a listen only mode.
After the speaker's introduction there will be a question and answer session.
To ask a question during the session you will need to press star 1 and 1 on your telephone.
You will then hear an automated message advising your hand is raised.
To withdraw your question please press star 1 and 1 again.
Please be advised that today's conference is being recorded.
I would now like to hand the conference call over to Mr. Jim Cavanaugh.
Please go ahead.
Thank you operator.
Welcome everyone.
This is Jim Cavanaugh head of investor relations at ASML.
Joining me today on the call are ASML CEO Christoph Fouquet and our CFO Roger Daffa.
The subject of today's call is ASML 2026 First Quarter Results.
The link to the call will be 60 minutes and questions will be taken in the order in which
they are received.
This call is also being broadcast live over the internet at www.ASML.com.
A transcript of management's opening remarks and replay of the call will be available on
our website shortly following the conclusion of this call.
Before we begin I would like to caution listeners that comments made by management during this
conference call will include forward looking statements within the meaning of the federal
securities laws.
These forward looking statements involve material risks and uncertainties.
For discussion of risk factors I encourage you to review the safe harbor statement contained
in today's press release and the presentation found on our website at www.ASML.com and in
ASML's annual report on form 20F and other documents as filed with the Securities and
Exchange Commission.
With that I would like to turn the call over to Christoph Fouquet for a brief introduction.
Mr. Jim welcome everyone and thank you for joining us for our First Quarter 2026 Resource
Conference call.
Before we begin the Q&A session, Fouquet and I would like to provide an overview and some
commentaries on the First Quarter 2026 results as well as provide some additional comments
on the current business environment and on our future business.
Thank you Christoph and welcome everyone.
I will first review the First Quarter 2026 financial accomplishments and then provide
guidance on the second quarter of 2026.
Let me start with our First Quarter accomplishments.
In the First Quarter of 2026 total net sales were 8.8 billion euros which is within our
guidance.
Net system sales were at 6.3 billion euros which includes over 4.1 billion euros from
EV system sales including sales from two high NA systems and over 2.1 billion euros
from non EV system sales.
Net system sales were almost equally split between logic at 49% and memory at 51%.
Installed base management sales for the quarter of K-Men at 2.5 billion euros slightly above
our guidance.
The first margin for the quarter was at the high end of our guidance at 53% primarily
due to the contribution of very high margin components within our installed base business.
On operating expenses we came in as guidance.
Our new expenses were rounded at 1.2 billion euros and as the GNA expenses came in at 0.3
billion euros.
The effective tax rates for Q1 was 17.1%.
For the full year 2026 the expected annualized effective tax rate is around 17%.
Net income in Q1 was 8.8 billion euros representing 31.4% of total net sales and resulting in
earnings per share of 7.15 euros.
Turning to the balance sheet we ended the First Quarter with cash, cash equivalents and
short-term investments at a level of 8.4 billion euros.
Our Q1 free cash grow was negative, 2.6 billion euros largely driven by the timing of down
payment.
With that I would like to turn to our expectations for the second quarter of 2026.
We expect Q2 total net sales to be between 8.4 billion euros and 9 billion euros.
We expect our Q2 installed base management sales to be around 2.5 billion euros.
First margin for Q2 is expected to be between 51 and 52%.
The expected R&D expenses for Q2 are around 1.2 billion euros and as the GNA is expected
to be around 0.3 billion euros.
Moving to our cash return to our shareholders in Q1 ASM outpaid a third interim dividend
over 2025 of 1.60 euro per ordinary share.
Is Malentans declared total dividend for the year 2025 of 7.50 euro per ordinary share
which is a 17% increase compared to 2024.
Recognizing the three interim dividends of 1.60 euro per ordinary share paid in 25 and
26, this needs to a final dividend proposal to the annual general meeting of 2.70 euro
per ordinary share.
In Q1 2026 we purchase shares for total amount of around 1.1 billion euros and with that I
would like to turn the call back over to Christoph.
Thank you, Roshi.
As Roshi highlighted we finished the first quarter with good financial reserves.
Looking ahead with respect to the market.
The growth outlook for the semiconductor industry continues to solidify driven primarily
by AI related infrastructure investment.
These investments are increasing demand for advanced logic and member of chips in many
areas and for the foreseeable future demand will continue to outpace supply.
This creates constraints across end markets from AI to mobile and PC's which is driving
our customers to aggressively add capacity.
In the memory business many customers have called that they are sold out for the remaining
of the year and that they expect the supply limitation to persist beyond 2026 despite
their plans to add significant capacity.
In the logic business our customers are adding capacity across multiple advanced nodes to
support demand while continuing to ramp the two nanometer node in support of next generation
HPC and mobile application.
They also expect supply limitation across those advanced nodes beyond 2026.
Also memory and logic customers are responding to this unprecedented demand by increasing
capital expenditures and accelerating capacity expansion plans this year and beyond.
Those investments are supported by long term agreement with their own customer.
In addition to expanding capacity both advanced DRAM and logic customers continue to further
adopt EV and immersion DPV on new process nodes which further increases their demand
for lithography.
As a resource ISMR order intake continues to be very strong and we stay closely aligned
with our customers to support their needs.
At the same time we offer customers productivity upgrade for their install base to increase
their short term output requirements.
Turning to our capacity expansion plan for 2026 we are executing on an output plan of
at least 60 low NA UV system and despite a slow start as discussed last quarter driving
output of our immersion system close to that in 2025.
Based on these developments in demand and order intake we are quarter by quarter increasing
the move rates for our HPM product raising low NA UV capacity to at least 80 system next
year and scaling DPV and application products in alignment.
We continue to see a strong year ahead and expect ISMR to grow in 2026.
Therefore, we are updating our 2026 guidance both narrowing and increasing the expected
revenue range to between 36 billion euros and 40 billion euros while maintaining our expectation
of a gross margin between 51% and 53%.
We are revenue weighted to the second half of the year.
As we said last quarter last quarter UV revenue is expected to raise significantly this year
driven by the dynamics in advanced logic and the RAM markets mentioned earlier.
For non-EU revenue we previously expected this to be similar to last year but given continued
demand momentum we now expect gross as a resource of customer adding more DPV lithography to
support their expansion plans.
We also expect install base management revenue to grow significantly this year driven by
service revenue from our expanding UV install base and customer demand for performance
upgrades to support their increasing capacity requirements.
We expect that the bandwidth in our 2026 guidance accommodates potential outcomes of ongoing
discussions around export control.
Turning to technology we continue to make very good progress on this front with several
developments recently highlighted at the STIE Advanced lithography and Patterning Conference
this February in San Jose.
At the conference we presented an updated low NAIV productive roadmap that reflects
improvement to both our short term and long term plans for these products.
This includes the ability to reach at least 330 wafer per hour on low NAIV at the start
of the next decade enabled in large part by our continued source powered improvement
as evidenced by our recent 1000 WAT source demonstration.
In the short term all NxE3800 E system can benefit from an upgrade that provides an increase
of 10 wafer per hours with 230 wafer per hours available immediately to all customer.
For NxE3800 F system we have raised the wafer per hour specification from 250 wafer
to 260 wafer per hours.
We plan to start shipping that system in 2027 with full volume slated for 2028.
We also shared that the INE platform has now processed over half a million wafers and
achieved over 80% availability.
So at the conference we saw customers presenting several strong papers highlighting the progress
with INE.
These presentations demonstrated use cases in both logic and in DRAM in which a single
INE exposure can replace complex multi-patterning processes that today require free or fall
low NA exposures.
For some critical layers INE can reduce the number of opposite steps by a factor of 10.
The progress being made throughout the ecosystem especially with resist allows us to target
line and speed pitches of 18 nanometer for logic and contact pitches below 28 nanometer
for DRAM.
This means that INE can support single exposed for at least three nodes in logic and DRAM.
We continue to work to further mature the platform alongside our customer as they start
to test the technology on product wafers and move closer to bringing INE into high volume
manufacturing.
Looking more broadly what we have seen over the past few months has further confirmed
our view on the positive impact of AI on customer demand for our advanced product and
especially for our EVC stem in both advanced memory and logic.
We see the end market dynamic supporting a shift in product mix towards more demand
for our advanced lithography product and an increase in nether intensity.
Our strong productivity on map on low NA in combination with the introduction of INE supports
for the cost of technology reduction for our customer.
In summary demand remains strong for the entire product portfolio.
With that we will be happy to take your questions.
Thank you Raje and thank you Christoph.
The operator will instruct you momentarily on the protocol for the Q&A session.
Beforehand I would like to ask you to kindly limit yourself to one question with one short
follow up if necessary.
This will allow us to get through as many callers as possible.
Now operator could we have your final instructions and then the first question please.
Thank you.
As a reminder to ask a question you will need to press star one and one on your telephone
and wait for your name to be announced.
To withdraw your question please press star one and one again.
We will now take the first question and your first question today comes from the line of
Joe Khatriki from Wells Fargo.
Please go ahead.
Yeah thanks for taking the question.
Maybe I just start with the updated 2026 revenue guide.
Do you talk about taking up your emerging outlook and your DUV outlook?
How much of that is coming from China versus non-China and did the EUV outlook change at
all?
It's a high growth for Jay.
This is primarily non-China when it comes to emerging.
China remains at the midpoint around 20%.
That's the expectation that we have for the China business so that view has not changed.
As we mentioned on the last call we were looking at immersion and said given the supply
chain situation and immersion we said we doubt whether we can get immersion to the level
that we had last year.
We've been working extremely hard and right now we're at a point where we believe we can
get immersion close to the levels that we had last year.
That would primarily flow to the non-Chinese customer.
That's where that is.
That's a major driver of the uptick and also in EUV.
We believe there is a bit more that can be done.
It's in that combination and as you can see from our achievements and also our expectations
for the insult based business that one is strong as well.
I would say it's actually a combination but clearly immersion is an important part of
the uptake of the guidance.
That's helpful.
Then just as a follow up on the gross margin, the range not changing but clearly immersion
increasing.
I know that's a bit higher gross margin.
Just any put and take there while the gross margin guides say it's the same for 26.
Yeah, you're right.
I mean for the entire year the gross margin remains in spite of a bit more immersion.
But it's also clear that we're increasing the move rate quite a bit.
Of course increasing the move rate means that you're hiring people, hiring people, training
people that also cost a bit of money.
So every ramp you always have a little bit of cost before you have the benefit of that.
That's the other side of the equation.
That's why we believe that the guidance range as we have it taken into consideration all
the puts and takes, we believe keeping it as we articulated it as the water would go
is the right way to go.
Thank you.
You're welcome.
Thank you.
Your next question comes from the line of Chris Sankar from TD Cowan.
Please go ahead.
We have time to take my question.
I do have them first run, Rosie O'Kirstoff.
I'm kind of curious, what is the visibility you're getting from your customers?
Does that extend into 2028?
Based on how to think about ASM as good profile in 27 and 28, either qualitatively or quantitatively?
Well, I think 2028 is still quite far away.
And I think a lot of the discussion we have with our customer today are still a bit about
As Rosie mentioned, we're still working hard even for 2026 with our customer to make sure
we deliver what they need.
And of course, a lot of discussion are moving to 2027, which is also why we are also there
working very hard to increase the capacity.
But 2028 is a bit too far away, I think.
Is there a way to think about 27 at this moment, Chris Starfall?
Well, I think we mentioned already that we are growing our move rate every quarter.
So I think we are still in a dynamic where we believe that we have to create more space
for our customer.
I think we've explained in our introduction that we are preparing to build at least 80
low-end U.V. system next year.
So I think these are the sign, if you need any, that of course the discussion we have
about 2027 are currently around making sure that we have enough capacity for our customer.
Carl, another quick follow-up.
I think you mentioned how you are undershipping demand today.
If you start looking at 2780 unit capacity for low NA, are you meeting demand?
Are you still undershipping demand at 80 units next year?
Well, I think on those discussion, and I think the number we share today are, I will
say, the results of very close discussion with our customers.
We talked about at least 80 because those discussions are still ongoing.
I think you have seen, of course, the dynamic in the last couple of quarters.
So I think we said that also last quarter.
We were working every day very closely with our customer to make sure basically that we
stay in line with what they need.
I think we have, of course, a very strong mission to meet their demand.
So that's what those number reflects.
I think that's the results of where our discussion are today.
Thank you, Kristoff.
You're welcome.
Thank you.
Your next question comes from the line of C.D.
Me is from Contell.
It's Gerald.
Please go ahead.
Yeah, good morning, Good afternoon.
Thank you for taking the question.
I guess first question going back to Gross margins.
And if we isolate March versus June, March, your revenue to INA zero, I think it embedded
in June.
And so I get kind of the upgrades as a real positive for March, but I would have thought
the higher low in EUV and immersion would have led to higher gross margins.
I understand you're adding Edgout, but that still seems pretty severe.
So wondering if there's anything else in that mix that's driving that change.
No, there's nothing in the mix really CJ.
It really is down to the elements that you just referred to.
So a few put on takes, puts on takes, but the primary ones being the very high margin
elements in the upgrade business that we had in Q1.
We're not counting on such a strong pattern in the upgrade business in Q2.
So that's an important element.
And then indeed, to the point that I made earlier on, the increase in hub counts that
were going through to prepare for optics for upgrades in the move rate.
So it's pretty low elements as a result of which we're now narrowing the bandwidth for
this particular quarter to 51 and 52.
Of course, you would see that the second half of the year, that volume is more skewed
towards the second half of the year, right?
If you look at our adorac items, so it's also appropriate to see more of the benefits
of the increase of the ramp that we're doing in that in the second half of the year.
As a result of that, we maintain the guidance for the full year at 51 to 53%.
Perfect mix, and I guess as a follow up, Kristoff, to try to talk about the supply side, obviously
under shipping and demand today.
Curious, how you working internally at ASML outside of headcount?
What kind of things are you doing to help drive productivity and extra supply and also
with your supply chain, particularly around the optics and mirror fronts?
What is necessary there?
And if you are considering 80 plus EUV, what does that mean from an immersion unit perspective
in 27?
Thanks so much.
I think we're working on many, many fronts, I would say.
You mentioned the supply chain, I think.
We have been mostly explaining the last few years that we were preparing the supply chain
basically to be able to go to a capability of 90 and low NA and to a capability of 604
for DPUV, total DPUV.
And I think what we see in these frames is that a lot of the preparation is paying off.
So I think that, of course, you always have challenge with the supply chain, but I will
say so far, also, our supply chain has been able to support our move rate increase quarter
by quarter.
And that's included by the way, Zeiss, to name them.
That's included the optic, where I think we had major challenges a few years ago in the
previous frames.
I think we are in a much better shape there.
I think that's also true with what we have prepared in terms of capacity here in ISML.
So I'm talking about the space we need to build the tools, both EUV and DPUV.
And this allows, as Roger mentioned, that to basically now add the people we will need
to meet the move rate that is required.
The other element I'd like to stress, we also talked a lot about the 38 and the E in
the last few years.
As you have noticed, the matching tool is a much better place than it was a year ago,
which also allows us to make great progress on cycle time in our factory, and of course,
a shorter cycle time gives us opportunity for more tools.
So we have many, many parameters that we drive, I would say, all at the same time,
very aggressively, basically, to get more output.
That's true for EUV.
That's true for DPUV.
